1:
The scene is fixed with care by the grammatical-historical witnesses. Keil and Delitzsch insist that the phrase rendered 'by the word of the LORD' is really 'in the word of Jehovah' — the word conceived not as a mere errand but as a power that comes upon the prophet and drives him to speak; Ellicott agrees that the man 'came clothed in the inspiration of the word put into his mouth,' and faults the weaker English rendering. Jeroboam, meanwhile, is caught in the very act, standing at the altar to burn incense as in ch. 12:33.

Much ink is spent on the man's identity, and here the voices converge in a shared refusal to guess. Gill notes that Josephus calls him Jadon and that the Jewish writers generally think him Iddo, but he rules Iddo out: Iddo lived on to chronicle Rehoboam and Abijah (2 Chron. 13:22), whereas this man was quickly slain; Shemaiah is excluded for the same reason. Ellicott traces the Iddo tradition to a mistaken reading of the 'visions of Iddo against Jeroboam,' and Clarke closes the matter bluntly: the Chaldee, Syriac and Arabic call him simply a prophet, the Hebrew a 'man of God,' one wholly devoted to God's service, and 'conjecture is idle on such a subject.'

The puritan Matthew Henry turns from the name to the mercy. The army of Judah that would have crushed Jeroboam was countermanded (ch. 12:24); instead God sends a single prophet to reclaim him, and sends him 'in time, while he is but dedicating his altar, before his heart is hardened by the deceitfulness of his sin,' for God delights not in the death of sinners. Henry marvels at the messenger's courage in confronting a proud king at the height of his solemnity, and notes the pointed address: the word is cried not to Jeroboam nor to the people but to the altar itself, whose stones 'would sooner hear and yield than those who were mad upon their idols and deaf to divine calls.' Yet in threatening the altar God threatens its founder and worshippers, who might well conclude that if his wrath fasten on the guiltless stones, how shall they escape. The witnesses do not divide; together they set the stage — an anonymous but Spirit-driven man of God, a warning of mercy, and a king about to reveal his heart.
2:
The prophet cries against the altar and names, three centuries in advance, the king who will defile it. The witnesses read the verse chiefly as a demonstration of divine foreknowledge, and they gather around the particularity of the prediction. The wesleyan Adam Clarke calls it 'one of the most remarkable and most singular prophecies in the Old Testament': a fact foretold some three hundred and forty years before it happened, attested by two nations who had every motive to test it — the Jews to guard it, the Israelites to expose it as false, had that been possible — and fulfilled to the letter when Josiah desecrated the altar with the priests' own bones (2 Kings 23:15-20). The prediction, Clarke says, put idolatry itself on notice, bidding it beware every king named Josiah.

The grammatical-historical voices weigh the naming more analytically. Ellicott sets it beside the well-known naming of Cyrus in Isa. 44:28, and observes that 'Josiah' is itself significant — 'one healed or helped by Jehovah' — so the name is no idle detail. He frames the true issue: such particularity 'will be credible or incredible to us, according to the view which we take of the nature of prophetic prediction.' If prophecy is only the intuitive sagacity of a keen mind reading the germs of the future in the present, the detail is incredible; if it is a supernatural entrance into the mind of God, 'in whose foreknowledge all the future is already seen and ordained,' then it is merely unusual, and in no way impossible. Keil and Delitzsch add that the word falls upon the altar as a penal power, decreeing its eventual desecration.

Here the witnesses touch their one real disagreement. Ellicott records the suggestion that the words 'Josiah by name' may be a later marginal gloss, or the chronicler's insertion after the event, rather than part of the original utterance — and he grants the latter 'in itself not unlikely.' Yet he does not press it, since the parallel of Cyrus and the significance of the name tell the other way. Matthew Henry, for his part, will have none of the softening: nothing is more contingent than the giving of a name, yet Josiah was named above three hundred years before his birth, for 'nothing future is hidden from God,' and there are 'names written in heaven' (Phil. 4:3). Beneath the debate all confess the same wonder — that the God who rules history had already written its verdict on Jeroboam's altar.
3:
To seal his word the prophet gives a sign: the altar shall be rent and its ashes poured out. The witnesses read the sign as a pledge and a rebuke, and though the comment here is brief they draw a full lesson from it. Gill notes that the sign is given 'as a confirmation of the truth of what the prophet had said, and for the proof of his being a true prophet of the Lord,' and Clarke agrees that it is 'a miracle to prove that the prophecy should be fulfilled in its season.' A word about a king three hundred years unborn could not be verified by the hearers; a present wonder could, and it vouched for the distant one.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fasten on the meaning of the act. The ashes are 'the ashes of the fat of the sacrificial animals,' and their pouring out, in consequence of the altar's breaking, is 'a penal sign, which indicated, along with the destruction of the altar, the desecration of the sacrificial service performed upon it.' The sign is thus not arbitrary but eloquent: what is scattered is the very worship Jeroboam was in the act of offering, declared unclean at its source. For how, the witnesses ask, could the gift be acceptable when the altar that should sanctify it was itself an abomination?

Matthew Henry gathers the fullest reflection, reading the promised sign at four levels. It was a proof that the prophet was sent of God, who 'confirmed the word with this sign following' (Mark 16:20); a present token of God's displeasure against the idolatrous sacrifice; a reproach to a people 'whose hearts were harder than these stones and rent not under the word of the Lord'; and a specimen in miniature of the ruin Josiah would one day bring — 'it was now rent, in token of its being then ruined.' The witnesses do not contend here; with one mind they take the rending of dead stone as God's answer to hearts that would not rend, a small immediate judgment guaranteeing a greater one to come, which the narrative reaches in v.5.
4:
Jeroboam's response exposes his heart: hearing the sentence, he stretches out his hand and cries 'Lay hold on him' — and the hand withers where it is thrust out. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe the miracle precisely: the hand 'dried up,' that is, 'became rigid in consequence of a miraculous withdrawal of the vital energy,' so that the king 'experienced in the limbs of his own body the severity of the threatened judgment of God.' Clarke pictures the moment vividly — Jeroboam no doubt stretching out his own hand in 'rage, pride, and haste' to seize the prophet — and notes the clinical result: 'the whole arm became suddenly rigid; the nerves no longer communicated their influence, and the muscles ceased to obey the dictates of the will.'

The puritan Matthew Henry draws out the moral drama with his characteristic force. Instead of trembling at the message, Jeroboam 'assaulted him that brought it, in defiance of the wrath of which he was warned and contempt of that grace which sent him the warning' — proof of the old rule that if you 'rebuke a sinner he will hate thee, and do thee a mischief if he can.' The withering is at once punishment and rescue: it disabled Jeroboam so that 'he could neither hurt the prophet nor draw it in to help himself,' God restraining the wrath of man to shield his servant. Henry catches the sharp irony — 'when his hand was stretched out to burn incense to his calves it was not withered; but, when it is stretched out against a prophet,' it fails — for of all wickedness none is 'more provoking to God than their malicious attempts against his prophets,' of whom he has said, Touch them not.

Henry alone lifts the judgment to a still more dreadful figure. The withered hand that Jeroboam 'could not pull in again' is an emblem of a spiritual doom: 'if God, in justice, harden the hearts of sinners, so that the hand they have stretched out in sin they cannot pull in again by repentance, that is a spiritual judgment, represented by this, and much more dreadful.' The witnesses agree without dispute: the outstretched, paralysed arm is the whole man in miniature — bold to strike at God's word, powerless to undo his own sin, and now, in v.6, driven to beg the help of the very man he meant to seize.
5:
The promised sign now falls due: the altar is rent and the ashes poured out, exactly as the man of God had spoken 'by the word of the LORD.' The witnesses treat the verse as the visible seal upon everything said in vv.2-3, and they underline that the wonder came 'immediately.' Gill pictures the altar 'split into two parts perhaps, and one fell one way, and the other another,' while Clarke stresses that it 'split or clave of its own accord' — no hand touched it — 'and, as the split parts would decline at the top from the line of their perpendicular, so the ashes and coals would fall off, or be poured out.' The self-moving rupture is the point: an invisible power did what no man present would have dared.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch name the theological weight of the moment. This is 'the penal miracle announced in the word of Jehovah, i.e., in the strength of the Lord,' and it 'took effect immediately upon the altar.' The timing is not incidental. The word had gone out against altar and king together; the first stroke lands on the stone, and the second, already felt in Jeroboam's rigid arm, now presses him toward surrender. Keil observes that 'the defiant king was now obliged to entreat the man of God' — the very man he had ordered seized a moment before.

So the two signs of the chapter answer each other. The withered hand judged the worshipper in his own body; the shattered altar judges his worship at its centre. Together they compose a single argument the witnesses find irresistible: the God who can crack an altar without a blow and freeze a hand in mid-command is the God whose distant word about Josiah will as surely come to pass. Jeroboam's contempt has been met with power, and his power turned to helplessness; there is nothing left for him but to plead, which is precisely what the next verse records. The scattered ashes, Keil's 'penal sign,' lie on the ground as a standing rebuke — the ruined foreshadowing of a worship that will one day be ruined for good.
6:
Broken in body, the king who had cried 'Lay hold on him' now begs, 'Intreat the face of the LORD thy God, and pray for me, that my hand may be restored.' The witnesses mark the reversal of tone. Gill notes that the king answers 'not in a haughty, but humble manner; not as threatening, but supplicating.' The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the Hebrew idiom exactly: to 'soften the face of the Lord' is 'to stroke the face of God, i.e., to render it soft by intercession' (Exod. 32:11), and the healing 'took place at once at the intercession of the prophet.'

Clarke lingers on the two revealing words 'thy God.' Jeroboam says thy God, not mine, 'for Jeroboam knew that he was not his God, for he was now in the very act of acknowledging other gods, and had no portion in the God of Jacob.' The face of God, Clarke adds, 'is his favor,' and both miracles — the withering and the healing — 'were wrought to show the truth of the Jewish religion, and to convince this bold innovator of his wickedness, and to reclaim him from the folly and ruinous tendency of his idolatry.'

The puritan Matthew Henry reads the whole episode as a study in half repentance. 'That word of God which should have touched his conscience humbled him not, but this which touched his bone and his flesh brings down his proud spirit.' Two things Henry finds hopeful: Jeroboam looks for help 'not from his calves, but from God only,' and he seeks it 'not by his own sacrifice or incense, but by the prayer and intercession of the prophet, whom he had just now threatened.' Yet the hope is thin, for Jeroboam asks only that his hand be healed, not that his sin be pardoned or his heart changed — 'thus Pharaoh would have Moses to pray that God would take away this death only.' The prophet, 'as became a man of God, renders good for evil,' answering the man who sought his life with immediate prayer, and so teaching the blessing pronounced on those who learn of Christ to pray for their persecutors (Matt. 5:44). The witnesses agree: God wounded and God alone made whole, that Jeroboam, 'if he were not broken by the judgment, yet might be melted by the mercy' — though, as Henry sadly grants, 'the impressions wore off.'
7:
His hand restored, Jeroboam changes tactics, inviting the prophet home to refresh himself and receive a reward. The witnesses debate his motive, and most detect policy beneath the courtesy. The grammatical-historical Ellicott allows that the invitation 'may have been in part the mark of some impression made on the king, and an impulse of gratitude for the restoration of his withered hand' — much like Naaman's offer to Elisha (2 Kings 5:15), which was likewise refused. But he judges that 'it still savours of astute policy': for the prophet's acceptance of hospitality and reward 'would in the eyes of the people imply a condonation of the idolatrous worship,' undoing the impression his prophecy had made. Keil and Delitzsch agree that the aim was 'not to do him honour... but to gain him over to his side,' that he might 'render his threat harmless in the eyes of the people.'

Ellicott presses the deeper point: the invitation betrays 'a low idea of prophetic character and mission,' like Balak's handling of Balaam, treating the man of God as one whose word might be bought or softened by favour. Such a notion, he notes, is 'perfectly compatible with a certain belief in the reality of a supernatural power in the prophet' — witness the monstrous offer of Simon Magus (Acts 8:19) — and it was precisely 'to provide against these things... that the prohibition of v.9 was given.'

The puritan Matthew Henry reads the human comedy in it. Jeroboam, so affected by the cure that we hear nothing of any thanksgiving to God or offering sent to Jerusalem, is nonetheless 'willing to express his gratitude to the prophet and pay him for his prayers,' for 'favours to the body will make even graceless men seem grateful to good ministers.' Gill adds only that the prophet had earned a meal, 'after such a journey he had taken, and delivered his prophecies, and put up his prayers for the king.' The witnesses do not finally divide; whether they weigh the invitation toward a flicker of gratitude or a stroke of statecraft, all see that a reward accepted here would have blunted the whole errand, and so they prepare the reader for the refusal that follows.
8:
'If thou wilt give me half thine house, I will not go in with thee.' The prophet's refusal is absolute, and the witnesses read it as obedience raised to the level of testimony. Gill is careful to guard the man's courtesy: the refusal was offered 'in answer to his civility... to assure him it was not out of contempt to him, or ill will to him, or slight of his favour, but in obedience to the will of God.' The 'no' is not rudeness but fidelity.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the reason God had attached to the ban. The prohibition against eating and drinking was meant 'to manifest His detestation of idolatry, and to show by that fact that the Bethelites were so detestable, and as it were excommunicated by God, that He wished none of the faithful to join with them in eating and drinking' — a judgment Keil borrows from Cornelius a Lapide. To break bread in Bethel would be to grant it fellowship; the fast is a visible verdict. Keil also flags what will soon matter: the same charge forbade the prophet to return by the road he came, 'that no one might look out for him' and delay him.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws the searching application. The prophet, 'though hungry and weary, and perhaps poor, in obedience to the divine command refused both the entertainment and the reward.' He might have reasoned that accepting would win him further access to a newly humbled king; 'yet he will not think himself wiser than God, but, like a faithful careful messenger, hastens home when he has done his errand.' Henry's verdict is a two-edged sword he will turn on this same man before the chapter ends: 'those have little learned the lessons of self-denial that cannot forbear one forbidden meal.' Ellicott, meanwhile, hears in the very firmness of the language an ominous note, 'strangely like the reluctant refusal of Balak's offer by Balaam' (Num. 22:18), remarking that 'the very strength of the language is suspicious.' The witnesses stand united on the deed while quietly preparing the reader: a man who could refuse a king may yet be undone by a subtler snare.
9:
The prophet cites his warrant: 'For so it was charged me by the word of the LORD, Eat no bread, nor drink water, nor turn again by the same way that thou camest.' The witnesses agree on the first two clauses and puzzle over the third. Gill and Clarke take the ban on food and drink as a command to have 'no kind of communication with those idolaters,' a total refusal of fellowship with 'the works of darkness.' Keil and Delitzsch note that the return by a different road served 'that no one might look out for him, and force him to a delay which was irreconcilable with his commission.'

The clause forbidding the old road draws the verse's small debate. Clarke offers a prudential reading: the prophet was to avoid the way he came 'lest the account of what was done should have reached the ears of any of the people,' who might persecute him or delay him with questions; yet he honestly confesses that 'what the reason of this part of the charge was, if not the above, is not easy to see.' The grammatical-historical Ellicott admits the command's significance is 'less obvious' and proposes a symbolic sense: the prophet 'should not suffer the way of his return... to be known, but should vanish swiftly, like the messenger of Elisha to Jehu' (2 Kings 9:3) 'when his work was done.' Ellicott then draws the line that governs the rest of the story: 'if so, his neglect of the spirit of the command was the first step in the way of his destruction.'

That last remark is the hinge of the chapter, and the witnesses set it deliberately. The threefold charge — no bread, no water, no returning — was a hedge of total separation, marking the man of God off from a place under judgment. Each clause said, in its own way, keep no company with Bethel, contract no delay, leave no trace. Gill stresses that all rested on 'the command of the Lord, by which he came to Bethel, and cried against the altar there.' The prophet repeats the words faultlessly here; the tragedy the witnesses see coming is not that he forgot the charge but that he will let another man persuade him to lay it down. For the moment he is obedient in every point, and v.10 records that obedience carried out to the letter.
10:
'So he went another way, and returned not by the way that he came to Bethel.' The verse is short and has drawn little comment, but the single witness who speaks of it makes it carry real weight. Gill observes that the man of God 'neither ate nor drank with the king, though that is not expressed; nor did he go back the same way he came; but in each particular observed the divine command, and was obedient to it.' Every clause of the threefold charge in v.9 is here honoured — no meal, no drink, no retracing of steps — and the narrator's silence about the food only underlines how complete the obedience was.

The importance of the verse is largely one of contrast. Standing between the prophet's refusal of a king and his fatal yielding to a fellow prophet, this quiet report of a road faithfully changed is the high-water mark of his obedience. He has met the full pressure of royal flattery and reward and come through untouched; he has kept not only the letter but, for the moment, the spirit of his charge, choosing the harder, longer, unfamiliar road home simply because God had said so. The man who 'went another way' is a man wholly in the right, and the reader is meant to feel the ground as solid beneath him.

That is precisely why the tragedy to come strikes so hard. The verse marks the last point at which the prophet is entirely safe, and the witnesses who reflect on the chapter as a whole read his ruin as the more sobering because it began from such firm footing. He was not overcome by the open enemy or the obvious temptation; he had already, as v.9 required, begun to vanish from the scene of judgment, obedient in every outward act. The 'another way' is thus both a commendation and a warning: obedience carried this far can still be surrendered if a man will take his directions from any mouth but God's own. For now, though, the verse simply records a servant doing exactly what he was told, on a road he would never have chosen, and it invites the reader to honour him before it asks him to mourn.
11:
A new figure enters — 'an old prophet in Bethel' — and the witnesses divide sharply over what manner of man he was. Gill frankly hesitates: 'it is hard to say what he was, a good man or a bad man.' Against him stand his dwelling in an idolatrous place, his suffering his sons to attend the idol-worship, and 'telling the man of God a premeditated lie'; for him stand his very title, that he 'did not attend idolatrous worship,' 'showed great respect to the man of God, had the word of God sent unto him... buried the man of God in his own grave, and desired his sons to bury him with him.' Gill notes the Jewish traditions naming him Micah, or (without foundation) Amaziah the priest of Bethel, or Gershom the son of Moses.

The puritan Matthew Henry has no such doubts and reads him as a bad man outright: 'I cannot but call him a false prophet and a bad man,' since 'a good tree could never bring forth such corrupt fruit.' He supposes one 'trained up among the sons of the prophets' who, 'growing worldly and profane,' had lost the spirit of prophecy while keeping the name; had he been good, 'he would have reproved Jeroboam's idolatry, and not have suffered his sons to attend his altars.' Henry even ties him to 'the prophet that came out of Samaria' (2 Kings 23:18), likening him to those later prophets who 'caused God's people Israel to err' (Jer. 23:13).

The grammatical-historical Ellicott and the wesleyan Clarke take a subtler middle path. Clarke supposes him 'probably once a prophet of the Lord, who had fallen from his steadfastness, and yet not so deeply as to lose the knowledge of the true God' — significantly, 'we find he was not at the king's sacrifice.' Ellicott insists the narrative's whole force depends on his being no mere pretender: 'like Balaam, he united true prophetic gifts with a low worldliness of temper, capable on occasion of base subterfuge and deceit.' He had 'thrown in his lot with Jeroboam's policy,' yet 'desired to continue still a prophet of the Lord.' Ellicott contrasts him with the genuine prophets who 'rebuke the sin' but 'do not forsake their ministry to the sinner' (ch. 14:9); the old prophet, by contrast, 'deliberately supports the idolatry, and that by the worst of falsehoods — a falsehood in the name of God.' The debate is real and unresolved, but its fruit is shared: this man's mingling of true gift and worldly compromise is, Ellicott says, 'only too characteristic of man's self-contradictory nature.'
12:
'What way went he?' The old prophet's question, put to his sons who had witnessed the day's events, sets the pursuit in motion, and the witnesses read it as the first move of a plan whose motive remains in doubt. Gill sees the old man asking the road 'having a strong inclination to see him, and converse with him.' Keil and Delitzsch supply the narrative texture: 'his sons had seen what way the man of God went,' and on their report the father calls at once for his ass to be saddled that he might ride after him.

The puritan Matthew Henry frames the ambiguity that the bare question leaves open. On the kinder reading, 'one may hope that he did it in compassion to him, concluding he wanted refreshment, and out of a desire to be better acquainted with him and more fully to understand his errand than he could from the report of his sons.' But Henry cannot rest there, for the sons had told their father everything — 'particularly that the prophet was forbidden to eat or drink there, which he had openly told Jeroboam.' Knowing the prohibition and still riding out to overturn it, the old prophet acts, Henry concludes, 'with a bad design, to draw him into a snare, and so to expose him.'

The question thus does more than ask directions; it exposes the deceiver's knowledge. Whatever curiosity or hospitality mingled in his heart, he set out fully aware that the man he pursued was under an express charge to take neither food nor drink in that place. The witnesses note the grim pattern Henry names elsewhere — that 'false prophets have ever been the worst enemies to the true prophets' — and they see it beginning here in a deceptively ordinary domestic scene: a father questioning his sons at day's end, an ass saddled, a road pointed out. The very smallness of the moment is part of the warning. The man of God had withstood a king armed with threats and rewards; he is about to be reached by an old man on a borrowed errand, asking only which way he went. Keil's plain report and Henry's darker reading together prepare the reader for the deception that v.18 will spring.
13:
'Saddle me the ass.' The old prophet's terse command to his sons is, for the witnesses, a small window onto the deliberateness of his errand. Gill reads the plain sense: the animal 'which he used to ride on,' the old man 'intimating he had a mind to ride after him, and overtake him, as he might reasonably think he might, since the man of God was on foot.' The calculation is cool and unhurried — a rider will easily catch a walker — and it shows an intention already formed. Keil and Delitzsch carry the narrative forward in the same breath: the ass saddled, 'he rode thereon' and 'went after the man of God.'

The detail of the ass is worth dwelling on, for it will return to haunt the story. The beast now saddled to fetch the prophet back to a forbidden meal is the same kind of creature that will later stand, unnaturally still, beside his corpse; and the old prophet who now mounts in eager pursuit will before long saddle an ass again, this time to bring home a body. The witnesses, reading the chapter whole, feel the irony gathering even in so bare a verse: the instrument of the seduction becomes, in God's hand, part of the sign of judgment. The very ass now saddled to carry the man of God back to a forbidden table will one day stand, against its nature, guarding his corpse; and the reader who knows the end feels the shadow of it fall across this brisk command.

What the verse chiefly registers is resolve. There is no hesitation in the old prophet, no weighing of whether he ought to intercept a man he knows to be under a divine prohibition. He simply gives the order and mounts. Whatever his mingled motives — the curiosity and hospitality some allow, the darker design others suspect — the outward act is single-minded. Against this settled purpose the man of God, resting under his oak, has no defence prepared, for he expects no assault from that quarter. The saddled ass, then, is the quiet engine of the tragedy: an ordinary act of an ordinary morning, bending with unhurried certainty toward the moment when a lie will turn a faithful man aside. The narrative moves, in v.14, to the meeting itself.
14:
The old prophet overtakes the man of God 'sitting under an oak,' and the witnesses draw from the scene a picture of weariness, a note of antiquity, and a fresh turn of the debate over the pursuer's intent. Gill sees the traveller resting 'to shelter him from the heat, and being faint, hungry, and thirsty,' and recalls that 'the ancients of old made use of oaks for a covering, before houses were invented,' as Abraham pitched his tent by the oak of Mamre. The grammatical-historical Ellicott sharpens the botany — 'properly, the oak, or terebinth' — and hears in it 'an evident mark of the antiquity of the document from which the history is taken,' like the oak at Shechem (Gen. 35:4), the oak at Ophrah (Judg. 6:11), and the palm of Deborah (Judg. 4:5).

On the old prophet's purpose the witnesses part ways. Clarke pleads the charitable reading: 'I can hardly think that this was with any evil design.' Persuaded by his sons' account of the prophecy and its power, the old man simply 'wished to have a particular acquaintance with him, in order that he might get farther information relative to the solemn import of the prophecy'; the man of God, Clarke concludes, 'could not have been an object of the old prophet's malevolence.' Ellicott is less sure, and reads the same scene against the grain: the picture of a prophet already seated and resting hints, he thinks, at 'a needless loitering of the prophet of Judah on the way.' Taken alone, he grants, it need not; 'but in relation to the temper indicated in the whole story, the thing may be not improbable.'

Here the two threads of the chapter cross. If Ellicott is right, the man of God is already dallying where he should be hastening, so that the seducer finds him halted rather than in flight — a first slackening of the spirit of the charge in v.9. And the old prophet's blunt opening — 'Art thou the man of God that camest from Judah?' answered simply, 'I am' — sets the trap in the frankest terms. The witnesses do not settle whether malice or mere curiosity drove the rider; but they agree that a wearied, seated, unsuspecting traveller and a determined questioner are now face to face, and that the invitation of v.15 will follow at once.
15:
'Come home with me, and eat bread.' The old prophet's invitation echoes the king's in v.7 almost word for word, but the witnesses see at once that it is far more dangerous. Gill judges the offer sincere on its face: 'in which invitation no doubt he was hearty, and might have no ill design in it, only to have some conversation with him, as being a good man, and a prophet of the Lord, especially upon the subject of his prophecies at Bethel.' The very plausibility is the peril.

What makes this second invitation deadly where the first was harmless is the source. Jeroboam was an open idolater whose reward the man of God could refuse without a flicker; the old man of Bethel comes robed in the prophetic name, offering fellowship between two servants of the same Lord. The puritan Matthew Henry states the principle the whole episode is built to teach: 'God's people are more in danger of being drawn from their duty by the plausible pretences of divinity and sanctity than by external inducements; we have therefore need to beware of false prophets, and not believe every spirit.' The man who 'had resolution enough to refuse the invitation of the king... could not resist the insinuations of one that pretended to be a prophet.'

So the verse marks the change of weapon. Threats and gold had been turned aside; now comes kindness in holy dress, an appeal not to appetite or fear but to the fellowship of a shared calling. The bait is 'eat bread' — the very thing expressly forbidden — but it is offered by one who seems entitled to sit at table with a man of God, over talk of the day's great prophecy. The witnesses see the man of God's guard, proof against the court, beginning to lower before the study. Nothing false has yet been spoken; the invitation as it stands is only warm and reasonable. But it opens the door through which the lie of v.18 will come, and the prophet, who answered a king so firmly, is about to give a fellow prophet the same firm answer one last time before he yields. The chapter is quietly teaching that the subtlest assaults on obedience wear the livery of godliness.
16:
'I may not return with thee, nor go in with thee.' The man of God's answer to the old prophet repeats, almost exactly, his answer to the king, and the witnesses mark the steadiness of it. Gill gives the ground plainly: he refuses 'into the city, and into his house in it, being such an idolatrous place, and especially being forbidden of God.' The two reasons stand together — Bethel is unclean, and God has spoken — and for the moment the second holds firm.

The strength of the reply lies in its form. 'I may not... nor go in with thee' is not the language of preference but of prohibition: the man speaks as one bound. Matthew Henry, reflecting on this exchange, notes how the prophet's own settled resolution ran together with the divine command — 'his resolutions concurring with the divine command' — so that when he says 'I may not, and therefore I will not, return to eat bread with thee,' conscience and commandment speak as one. It is the same wall that turned back Jeroboam's half-a-house; the man of God shows no sign of weakening, and answers the prophet with the identical firmness he had shown the throne.

That is what makes the scene so poignant to the witnesses who read the chapter whole. Everything in v.16 is right. The prophet knows the place is idolatrous, knows the command is express, and states both without hesitation. His refusal is not grudging but principled, resting where all his obedience has rested — on the plain word of the Lord. There is as yet no crack in the man; he has met the subtler temptation of holy fellowship exactly as he met the cruder temptation of royal favour, and held. The whole force of what follows depends on this: that the man about to fall is, at this moment, standing wholly upright, giving the correct answer for the correct reason. The witnesses let the verse register that uprightness so that the reader will feel the weight of its overthrow. In v.17 he will state his warrant once more — the word of the Lord that forbade him — and in v.18 that warrant will be met by a rival word, falsely claimed, that he ought never to have believed.
17:
'For it was said to me by the word of the LORD, Thou shalt eat no bread nor drink water there, nor turn again to go by the way that thou camest.' The man of God grounds his refusal, as he has grounded everything, on a direct word from God. Gill catches the force of the man's conviction: what was said to him he was 'persuaded came from the Lord himself, and therefore looked upon himself under obligation to regard' it. Keil and Delitzsch simply record the repetition of the whole threefold charge — no bread, no water, no returning by the same road — now rehearsed to the old prophet as it had been rehearsed to the king.

The witnesses find the deepest significance in a single phrase the man of God uses of his warrant, for it will stand in sharp contrast to what the old prophet claims in the next verse. The man of God says the command came 'by the word of the LORD' spoken to himself; his authority is first-hand, unmediated, certain. He does not appeal to an angel, or to another prophet, or to any report, but to what God had said directly to him. This is the very rock on which his obedience has been built and on which, so far, it stands unshaken.

And that is the tragedy the witnesses see approaching. In v.17 the prophet names the one sufficient ground of obedience — God's own word to God's own servant — and states it faultlessly. He has the truth exactly, holds it firmly, and repeats it without a tremor. What will undo him is not that he forgets this word but that he will let it be overruled by a different word, claimed at second hand by a stranger. The clause he leans on here, 'the word of the LORD,' is precisely the clause the old prophet will counterfeit in v.18 — 'an angel spake unto me by the word of the LORD' — and the man of God's fatal error will be to weigh a borrowed revelation against the one he received himself. For now, though, he is entirely in the right, quoting his charge whole, clinging to the direct word of God as the only thing that can bind a prophet's conscience. The witnesses set his correct principle in bold relief so that his coming failure to apply it may be felt for the loss it is.
18:
Now comes the lie: 'I am a prophet also as thou art; and an angel spake unto me by the word of the LORD, saying, Bring him back with thee into thine house, that he may eat bread and drink water.' The witnesses agree that the words were false, but they divide over whether the old prophet knew it. Clarke states the plain fact: 'that he lied unto him is here expressly asserted, and is amply proved by the event.' Yet Clarke cannot see clearly why, and canvasses several motives — sheer curiosity 'to know all about this prediction,' or even humanity, seeing the man 'faint with fatigue and fasting,' wishing to persuade him to take refreshment.

Then Clarke opens the harder possibility, weighing a distinction in the very words: 'is it not possible that the old prophet was himself deceived?' The man of God had said 'It was said to me by the word of the Lord,' but the old prophet says only, 'An angel spake to me by the word of the Lord.' Clarke thinks it 'very likely that an angel did appear to him' — 'an angel of darkness and idolatry, in the garb of an angel of light' — sent 'to bring discredit on the awful transactions which had lately taken place.' On this reading the deceiver may himself have been deceived, a fallen conscience easy prey to a lying spirit.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott will not soften the guilt, though he too locates it in self-deception. 'The lie was gross, and ought to have been obvious to one who had received a plain command,' who 'must have known that God was not a man that He should lie.' Why then was it believed? 'Because it chimed in with some secret reluctance to obey, and, by obedience, to give up all reward and hospitality. Hence the belief was a self-deceit, and, as such, culpable.' Ellicott finds nothing strange in the fatal outcome to any who know 'the self-deceiving colour which our wish gives to our thought,' and points the reader to Bishop Butler's famous sermon on self-deceit. The witnesses thus hold two things together: the word spoken was a lie in God's name — the gravest of falsehoods — and the man of God believed it not because it was credible but because he half-wished it true. The counterfeit revelation of v.18 succeeds only where a genuine one had already begun to be resented.
19:
'So he went back with him.' In four words the man of God undoes his obedience, and the witnesses concentrate on where, exactly, the sin lay. Gill is clear that 'in this he sinned,' and states the principle: having 'the command of God not to eat and drink in that place, he ought to have had the countermand from the Lord, and not trusted to another person.' Gill is generous in listing what might be pleaded for him — that the old man was an ancient prophet of the Lord, that he might think eating with a prophet different from eating with idolaters, that an angel's direction was alleged, that mercy might have relaxed the ban — 'but, after all, he ought to have taken no directions but from the Lord himself; in this he failed.'

The wesleyan Clarke reasons through the same problem at length and reaches the same verdict. He grants that the man might have supposed 'God, who had given him the charge, had authority to say' it was now relaxed — pointing to Abraham, bidden to offer Isaac and then stopped (Gen. 22:11), where God 'dispensed with the actual offering' once obedience was proved. So a plausible case can be made. 'But if this be so, why should he be punished with death?' Clarke's answer pinpoints the fault: 'He should not have taken a step back, till he had remission of the clause from the same authority which gave him the general message. He should have had it from the word of the Lord to himself, in both cases, as Abraham had; and not taken an apparent contradiction... from the mouth of a stranger.' In that alone, Clarke says, 'lay the sinfulness of the act.'

The puritan Matthew Henry names the broader lesson: the man 'that had resolution enough to refuse the invitation of the king... could not resist the insinuations of one that pretended to be a prophet.' Henry has already stated the rule the failure illustrates — believers are likeliest to be drawn from duty 'by the plausible pretences of divinity and sanctity.' The witnesses do not divide on substance: the man of God had a certain word from God and an uncertain word from a man, and he let the uncertain overrule the certain. His courtesy to a supposed elder, his hunger, his half-willingness to be persuaded — none of these could license him to obey any voice but the One that first sent him. He went back, and with that step the sentence of v.21-22 becomes inevitable.
20:
'As they sat at the table, the word of the LORD came unto the prophet that brought him back' — and here the witnesses meet the story's most disquieting turn: the sentence of doom is uttered by the very man who told the lie. The chief question is whether the word came through the old prophet's lips or directly from God. Clarke reports Dr. Kennicott's argument that the Hebrew of 'that brought him back' is ambiguous and may mean 'whom he had brought back,' the same phrase as in v.23; on that reading 'we are at liberty to consider the word of the Lord as delivered to the true prophet thus brought back,' God 'calling to him out of heaven, as in' the case of Abraham (Gen. 22:11). Clarke notes that v.26 says 'the Lord hath delivered him unto the lion, according to the word of the Lord which He spake unto him,' and that Josephus and the Arabic 'expressly assert that the sentence was declared by God to the true prophet.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott takes the harder, and to him the more instructive, view — that the doom really was spoken through the deceiver's mouth. 'It is, perhaps, the most terrible feature in the history that the Divine sentence is spoken... through the very lips which by falsehood had lured the prophet of Judah from the right path, and at the very table of treacherous hospitality.' He charges Josephus with a 'perverse tendency to explain away all that seems startling,' missing the point by assigning the revelation to the man of God himself. For Ellicott the scene is a parable of a standing law: 'that the voice of worldly wisdom first beguiles the servants of God to disobedience by false glosses on their duty to Him, and then proclaims unsparingly their sin and its just punishment.'

Gill, treating the same moment, feels the human weight of it: the old prophet's mind was 'greatly affected with the word,' both on his own account, since his lie had seduced the man, and on the other's, now threatened with death. The witnesses do not resolve the textual puzzle, and it matters less than it seems, for both readings confess the same God at work — whether he thundered from heaven to the true prophet as to Abraham, or bent the false prophet's tongue as he once bent Balaam's. Either way the deceived man now hears his fate, and the table of forbidden bread becomes the seat of judgment.
21:
'Thus saith the LORD, Forasmuch as thou hast disobeyed the mouth of the LORD, and hast not kept the commandment which the LORD thy God commanded thee.' The charge is read out, and the witnesses attend both to its content and to the strange emotion of the one who reads it. Keil and Delitzsch set out the indictment plainly: the man 'hast disobeyed the mouth of the LORD,' returning to eat and drink where God had forbidden it, and therefore 'thy carcase shall not come unto the sepulchre of thy fathers.' The offence is named exactly — not idolatry, not malice, but the single act of setting aside an express command.

Gill fixes on the cry itself, 'he cried unto the man of God.' The old prophet's mind was 'greatly affected with the word brought to him, partly on his own account, who, by a lie his conscience must accuse him of, had been the means of seducing the man of God, and partly on his account, who was threatened with death for complying with him.' The deceiver, in other words, is pierced by the very sentence he pronounces; the word that condemns his guest condemns his own falsehood in the same breath.

The wesleyan Clarke, holding to his reading of v.20, keeps the old prophet out of it altogether: on his view this is 'the voice of God from heaven addressing the man of God, the old prophet having nothing to do in this business.' Whether the doom passes through the deceiver's lips or over his head, the substance is the same, and the witnesses let it stand in its severity. What strikes them is the disproportion the chapter is pressing toward: a single meal, taken under deception, in a moment of weariness, draws a sentence of death and dishonoured burial, while the man who engineered the fall speaks on unpunished. The witnesses do not yet resolve that scandal — Henry and Clarke will wrestle with it at v.23 and v.24 — but here they let the bare word land: disobedience to the mouth of the Lord, however extenuated, is a grave thing, and God will not treat it lightly even in his own servant. The verse turns, in v.22, to spell out the penalty.
22:
'Thy carcase shall not come unto the sepulchre of thy fathers.' The penalty is spelled out, and the witnesses weigh both its meaning and its justice. Gill draws the pointed irony of the case: the man 'came back, and hast eaten bread and drunk water in the place' — the very command 'which he observed when first there, though invited by the king; and yet, after he had got out of the place, was prevailed upon to return and transgress.' He kept the charge under the greater pressure and broke it under the lesser; he stood before a throne and fell before a table.

The wesleyan Clarke reads the sentence for what it does and does not say: it 'intimated to him that he was to die an untimely death, but probably did not specify by what means.' The man of God is told that he will not reach home, that his body will be cut off from the honoured rest of his fathers' tomb, but not that a lion waits on the road; the manner is left hidden, the fact made certain. Matthew Henry unfolds the force of the words: 'Thou shalt never reach thy own house, but shalt be a carcase quickly, nor shall thy dead body be brought to the place of thy fathers' sepulchres, to be interred.' To an Israelite the denial of burial with his fathers was a signal disgrace, a public token of divine displeasure resting on the manner of his death.

The witnesses feel the weight of the disproportion and refuse to blunt it. The punishment is real, sudden, and severe, and it falls on a true servant of God for a single act of disobedience procured by another's lie. Henry insists this teaches 'that nothing is more provoking to God than disobedience to an express command, though in a small matter,' a truth that makes God's dealing with the first parents 'the easier to be accounted for.' Yet even in severity the witnesses will find mercy: the sentence touches the body and its burial, not the soul, and Clarke will argue at v.24 that this was a sin punished 'with the death of the body, while he extends mercy to the soul.' The verse leaves the reader with a hard but wholesome lesson — that God's honour is bound up with the obedience of those who bear his word, and that he will vindicate it, if need be, in the ruin of a prophet.
23:
The meal ended, the old prophet saddles the ass for the man he had fetched back, and the man of God rides out to meet his death. The witnesses read the quiet resumption of the story as the calm before judgment. Keil and Delitzsch note the plain sequence: 'after he had eaten he saddled the ass for him, i.e., for the prophet whom he had fetched back, and the latter departed upon it.' Gill remarks with wonder that the terrible word just spoken did not so much as spoil the meal: 'it seems this did not hinder their proceeding to make an end of their meal, which one would have thought would have spoiled their appetite.'

The puritan Matthew Henry gathers the fullest reflection, and takes the occasion to answer the scandal of the sentence. The man's offence, he insists, 'was great, and it would by no means justify him that he was drawn into it by a lie; he could not be so certain of the countermand sent by another as he was of the command given to himself.' He 'had great reason to suspect the honesty of this old prophet' and 'should have taken time to beg direction from God, and not have complied so soon.' To plead hunger is no defence: 'Had he never read that man lives not by bread alone?'

Henry then lifts the death to the plane of God's glory, finding in it three lessons. It shows 'that nothing is more provoking to him than disobedience to an express command, though in a small matter,' which makes his dealing with Adam over the forbidden fruit 'the easier to be accounted for.' It shows 'that God is displeased at the sins of his own people, and no man shall be protected in disobedience by the sanctity of his profession, the dignity of his office... or any good services he has done for him.' And it may even, Henry suggests, have been meant 'in a way of righteous judgment, to harden Jeroboam's heart,' who would seize on the prophet's fate as proof that meddling with his altar was dangerous work. The witnesses thus turn the offence of the verse into instruction: that God's severity toward his own servant is not caprice but a jealous guarding of his word, and a warning 'to all those whom he employs strictly to observe their orders, at their peril.' The lion of v.24 executes what the table has decreed.
24:
On the road 'a lion met him... and slew him,' and the corpse lay in the way with the ass and the lion standing by it. The witnesses read the death as both judgment and marvel. Keil and Delitzsch note that the lion, 'contrary to its nature,' neither devoured the man it had killed nor tore the ass — a restraint that marks the whole event as the direct work of God, who 'hath delivered him unto the lion... according to the word of the LORD.' The grammatical-historical Ellicott, ever the antiquarian, fills in the natural background: lions were common in old Palestine — at Timnath, near Bethlehem (1 Sam. 17:34), in the Jordan thickets (Jer. 5:6) — leaving their trace in place-names like Lebaoth, 'the house of lions'; 'now that the forests have disappeared from Palestine the lions have disappeared with them.'

The wesleyan Clarke faces the theological scandal head-on. This, he says, was 'the sin unto death; that is, such a sin as God will punish with the death of the body, while he extends mercy to the soul' (1 John 5:16-17). Here we see 'that often judgment begins at the house of God': the true prophet is slain and denied his fathers' grave, 'while the wicked king, and the old fallen prophet, are both permitted to live.' Clarke turns the apparent injustice inside out — 'if this was severity to the man of God, it was mercy to the others, neither of whom was prepared to meet his judge' — and closes with the awe of the apostle, 'if the righteous scarcely be saved, where shall the ungodly and the sinner appear?'

The puritan Matthew Henry, weighing the same disproportion, first underlines the offence — 'he could not be so certain of the countermand sent by another as he was of the command given to himself' — and then finds mercy woven into the wrath. The lion that killed him 'did not devour his dead body, nor so much as tear the ass,' nor fall upon the travellers who passed, nor upon the old prophet who came to fetch the corpse; 'his commission was to kill the prophet; hitherto he should go, but no further.' So God 'showed that, though he was angry with him, his anger was turned away, and the punishment went no further than death.' The witnesses agree in the end: the death is a real and dreadful judgment on a servant's disobedience, yet hedged about with signs of a mercy that reaches beyond the grave.
25:
'And, behold, men passed by, and saw the carcase cast in the way, and the lion standing by the carcase.' The single witness who comments points to the marvel that the whole scene depends on. Gill observes that as the travellers approached, the lion 'nor did the lion offer to seize on them, nor desert the carcass upon their approach' — it neither attacked the living nor abandoned the dead. A hungry beast that had just killed a man now stands guard over the body, indifferent to easy prey and unmoved by the coming of witnesses.

The unnaturalness is the message. Everything about the picture is against the grain of a lion's nature: it has not eaten the man it slew, has not touched the ass tethered beside it, and does not spring upon the travellers who draw near enough to see and count the strange tableau. The report they carry back to the city is therefore not merely news of a death but news of a portent — a body untouched, an ass unharmed, a lion standing sentinel. The reader is meant to understand that a power beyond the animal is holding it in place, that the lion is an instrument still under orders, its commission to kill fulfilled and its appetite restrained.

This is why the verse, brief as it is, carries the theological weight of the passage forward. The death of the man of God was no chance mauling by a wild beast but a measured judgment, and the standing lion is God's own signature upon it, distinguishing this killing from every ordinary one. The very travellers become witnesses to the supernatural restraint, and their testimony will bring the old prophet to the spot. In the standing lion the chapter holds together its two notes — severity and mercy — for the same beast that executed the sentence now preserves the body from being devoured, guarding it until it can be honourably buried. The men who pass by see, without knowing it, a scene arranged by God: judgment done, and yet the fallen servant's remains kept whole. Their report, in v.26, will draw from the old prophet the confession that names what has happened.
26:
Hearing the travellers' report, the old prophet knows at once what has happened: 'It is the man of God, who was disobedient unto the word of the LORD: therefore the LORD hath delivered him unto the lion.' Gill identifies the speaker simply as 'the old prophet, that had deceived him, by telling him a lie.' Keil and Delitzsch supply the full confession, in which the old man reads the death as fulfilment: the Lord 'hath delivered him to the lion, so that it hath torn him... and slain him according to the word of the Lord, which he spake unto him.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott catches the fine psychology of the moment. The old prophet 'did not know how his prediction was to be fulfilled, but recognised at once its supernatural fulfilment.' And Ellicott hears something telling in the way he speaks: 'there is in his words a characteristic reticence as to his own share in the work, in respect both of the deceit and the prediction of judgment.' He names the man's disobedience but says nothing of the lie that produced it, nor of his own mouth that pronounced the doom — a silence Ellicott takes as 'indicating something of the strange mixture of remorse and unscrupulous policy which comes out in his later action.'

The scene is heavy with irony, and the witnesses feel it. The one man who could have said, 'this death is my doing,' instead delivers a flawless theological verdict on his victim: the man was disobedient, and God has judged him. Every word is true, and yet it is spoken by the author of the disobedience, who edits himself out of his own account. The old prophet has become, in effect, the interpreter of a tragedy he caused, confessing God's justice while veiling his own guilt. There is no false note in his theology and no honesty in his self-portrait; the two hang together in exactly the 'self-contradictory' temper Ellicott has described from the first. What follows shows the remorse to be real enough in its way — he will fetch the body, mourn over it, and ask to share its grave — but it never rises to open confession. The verse leaves the reader with the disquieting spectacle of a man who knows the truth of God perfectly and the truth about himself not at all, and it moves, in v.27, to his errand of burial.
27:
'And he spake to his sons, saying, Saddle me the ass. And they saddled him.' For the second time the old prophet calls for a beast to be readied, and the witnesses note the quiet detail. Gill remarks that 'it seems he had more than one, for he had sent away the man of God with one before,' and this now is 'the ass he commonly rode on.' The same household that had saddled an animal to fetch the prophet to a forbidden meal now saddles another to fetch his corpse.

Keil and Delitzsch carry the errand through: the old man 'went and found the corpse and the ass standing by it, without the lion having eaten the corpse or torn the ass in pieces; and he lifted the corpse upon his ass, and brought it into his own city, and laid the corpse in his grave with the customary lamentation.' The whole sequence of burial is thus set in motion by this brief command, and Keil already looks ahead to the old prophet's charge to his sons and to his confidence that the word cried 'against the altar at Bethel and upon all the houses of the high places in the cities of Samaria will take place.'

The repeated saddling is more than narrative economy; it binds the two halves of the story together. The ass that bore the man of God away to his death now goes out to bring him back to his grave, and the old prophet who set the tragedy going by that first errand now labours to honour its victim. There is in the doubling a kind of rough penance: the hands that saddled for seduction saddle now for burial. The witnesses read in it the awakening of whatever conscience the old prophet had — a man who cannot undo the death he caused but will not leave the body dishonoured in the road. He rides out, as he rode out before, but the second journey reverses the first, exchanging the pursuit of a living prophet for the recovery of a dead one. The verse, small as it is, turns the story from judgment toward burial, and prepares the strange scene of grief and self-implication that v.28-31 will unfold.
28:
'And he went and found his carcase cast in the way, and the ass and the lion standing by the carcase: the lion had not eaten the carcase, nor torn the ass.' The witnesses gather here to marvel at the tableau, which the wesleyan Clarke sums up in a sentence: 'all here was preternatural.' The lion, 'though he had killed the man, does not devour him; the ass stands quietly by, not fearing the lion; and the lion does not attempt to tear the ass: both stand as guardians of the fallen prophet.' Clarke's conclusion is a cry of wonder — 'how evident is the hand of God in all!'

Keil and Delitzsch record the same scene with restraint, noting the corpse 'cast in the way' and the ass and lion posted beside it, the beast having neither eaten the man nor torn the animal. Gill draws out the boldness the sight gave the old prophet, who took up the body seeing that the lion 'did not attempt to devour the carcass, nor touch the ass, nor do any hurt to those that passed by.' Three unnatural restraints stand together: a predator that will not eat, a prey animal that will not flee, and a killer that will not kill again.

The theological point the witnesses press is the visibility of God's hand. Nature has been suspended in every direction at once, and the suspension is legible: the lion's appetite, the ass's terror, the beast's readiness to strike — all held in check by an unseen command, so that no passer-by could mistake this for an ordinary mauling. The scene is a signed judgment. Yet the same restraint that proves the death divine also tempers it with mercy, for the body that a lion would normally have torn to pieces lies whole, guarded until it can be carried home and honourably buried. The witnesses hold the two truths together as they have all along: God was angry enough to send the lion and merciful enough to muzzle it. The old prophet, reading the plain evidence of that hand, does not fear to approach; he lifts the carcase, and the narrative moves, in v.29, to the sorrowful journey back.
29:
'And the prophet took up the carcase of the man of God, and laid it upon the ass, and brought it back.' The single commenting witness fixes on the fearlessness with which the old prophet approached. Gill supposes that 'the lion perhaps made off as soon as he came, or, if he stayed, the prophet was not afraid of him, seeing he did not attempt to devour the carcass, nor touch the ass, nor do any hurt to those that passed by.' The evidence of the lion's supernatural restraint, plain to the travellers, was plain to the old prophet too, and it emboldened him to do what no man would dare beside a hungry predator: to walk up, lift the body, and load it on his beast.

The act itself is one of reverence. The old prophet does not leave the corpse to the road and the weather, nor to the beasts and birds; he gathers it up with his own hands and bears it home. Keil and Delitzsch, narrating the sequence, describe how he 'lifted the corpse upon his ass, and brought it into his own city' for burial and mourning. Whatever tangle of remorse and policy moved him, the outward deed is tender: the man he had deceived to death is carried back not as a criminal cast out but as a brother to be honoured.

The witnesses let the quiet reverence of the verse register the story's deepening strangeness. The same hands that saddled the ass for seduction now bear the body of their victim; the same road that carried the man of God to his death carries him back to a grave. And over it all lies the guardianship of God, who has kept the corpse whole precisely so that this honour could be done it. The preservation of the body, marvelled at through the preceding verses, finds here its purpose: not that the dead prophet might be displayed as a warning left to rot, but that he might be decently buried, his disobedience judged and his person still owned. The old prophet becomes, against every expectation, the instrument of that mercy, carrying to its rest the man his lie had undone. The narrative moves, in v.30, to the grave itself and the lament raised over it.
30:
'And he laid his carcase in his own grave; and they mourned over him, saying, Alas, my brother!' The witnesses attend to the honour done the dead and to the note of grief. Gill observes that the old prophet 'laid his carcass in his own grave, which he had prepared for himself; for, as he came from Samaria, it could not be the sepulchre of his fathers.' Denied by the sentence of v.22 the burial of his own fathers, the man of God is given instead the grave a stranger had readied for himself — 'showing all the respect, and doing all the honour to him, he well could.'

The wesleyan Clarke lingers over the lament. 'This lamentation is very simple, very short, and very pathetic.' He allows two readings of it: perhaps the old prophet said 'Alas, my brother' 'as much in reference to himself, who had been the cause of his untimely death, as in reference to the man of God whose corpse he now committed to the tomb'; or perhaps the words were simply 'the burden of each line of the lamentation which was used on this occasion' (Jer. 22:18). Keil and Delitzsch preserve the Hebrew cry, 'hoy achi' — alas, my brother — the customary funeral wail of the East.

The verse holds together grief and guilt in a single word. 'My brother' claims kinship with the man he had killed, and the mourning may well be as much for the mourner's own act as for the dead. There is something almost unbearable in the tenderness: the deceiver weeping over the grave he has caused to be dug, laying the body of his victim in the plot meant for his own bones. The witnesses do not resolve whether the sorrow is chiefly remorse or chiefly affection, and perhaps it is both, tangled past separating in a heart Ellicott has called self-contradictory. What is plain is that the old prophet spares no honour he can give — his own grave, a genuine lament, the language of brotherhood — as if to make what amends the living can make to the dead. Yet no amends can undo the death, and the grave itself will become, in the charge of v.31, the pledge of a further confession: that the man he mourns spoke truly, and that he means to lie beside him.
31:
'When I am dead, then bury me in the sepulchre wherein the man of God is buried; lay my bones beside his bones.' The old prophet's charge to his sons is, for the witnesses, the clearest sign of his mingled faith and fear. Gill records the plain instruction; the wesleyan Clarke reads its meaning: the request 'argues a strong conviction in the mind of the old prophet, that the deceased was a good and holy man of God; and he is willing to have place with him in the general resurrection.'

The motive is striking. The old prophet, who deceived a true servant to his death, now stakes his own hope of resurrection on being found at that servant's side. He believes the man of God vindicated — that whatever the shame of his end, he was and remains God's own, and his bones a pledge of blessing. By asking to lie beside them, the old prophet confesses more than his lament had said: not merely that he grieves, but that he judges the dead man righteous and himself in need of his company. Keil and Delitzsch, following the charge, connect it directly to the old prophet's certainty that the man's prophecy 'will be fulfilled,' so that the grave becomes a wager on the word.

There is a rough theology in the gesture that the witnesses do not miss. The old prophet cannot undo his sin, and he never openly confesses it; but he does the next thing, binding his own dead body to the body of the man he wronged, as though to say that he will rise or fall with him. It is a strange penitence — practical, indirect, self-interested even, seeking the safety of the righteous man's neighbourhood at the last day — and yet it carries a real submission to God's verdict. He honours the very prophecy he had contradicted, and clings in death to the servant he had ruined. The instruction also serves the narrative: because the two lie together, the tomb and its inscription survive to Josiah's day, when, sparing these bones alone, the reforming king will unwittingly seal the truth of the whole story (2 Kings 23:18). The old prophet's dying wish thus reaches forward three centuries, and the theme of the word's certain fulfilment gathers, in v.32, into open speech.
32:
The old prophet's confidence rests on a certainty: 'the saying which he cried by the word of the LORD against the altar in Bethel, and against all the houses of the high places which are in the cities of Samaria, shall surely come to pass.' The witnesses read the verse as a ringing affirmation of the prophecy's reliability, but they pause over a famous difficulty — the phrase 'cities of Samaria,' since Samaria did not yet exist. Clarke states the problem: Samaria was founded by Omri, who bought the hill from Shemer and named the city after him (ch. 16:24), 'and this was fifty years after the death of Jeroboam. How then could the old prophet speak of Samaria, not then in existence?'

Two answers divide the witnesses. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take the phrase as the narrator's, not the prophet's: 'the expression cities of Samaria belongs to the author of these books, and is used proleptically of the kingdom of the ten tribes,' named by anticipation from the capital that would later give it its name. On this reading the old prophet spoke only of the high places, and the historian, writing afterward, supplied the familiar regional term. Clarke lays this solution alongside a second: that the historian added the words knowing they fit his own day, 'for himself... gives us the account of its foundation by Omri.'

But Clarke will not close the door on a bolder possibility — that the words were genuinely prophetic. 'After all, it is possible that God might have given this revelation to the old prophet; and thus by anticipation, which is the language of prophecy, spoke of Samaria as then existing.' He appeals to the very pattern the chapter began with: as the man of God 'called Josiah by name three hundred years before he was born,' so a prophet might name Samaria before it was built, prophecy freely 'calling things that are not as though they were.' This, Clarke notes, is 'the solution of Houbigant, and is thought sound by many good critics.' The witnesses do not finally settle the point, and they need not, for both readings honour the verse's real burden: whether by the narrator's proleptic pen or by the prophet's God-given foresight, the word cried at Bethel is sure, and it 'shall surely come to pass.' Josiah's later fulfilment (2 Kings 23:15-20) proves the confidence well placed.
33:
'After this thing Jeroboam returned not from his evil way, but made again of the lowest of the people priests of the high places.' The witnesses read the verse as the tragic sum of the chapter: neither sign nor sentence moved the king. Gill lays out the catalogue of mercies and warnings that failed — Jeroboam 'had seen his altar rent, and the ashes poured out... his own hand withered, and that restored again upon the prayer of the prophet,' and had heard of the man of God's death; yet 'these were so far from reforming him, that he seemed to be the more hardened thereby.'

The wesleyan Clarke names the pathology. 'There is something exceedingly obstinate and perverse, as well as blinding and infatuating, in idolatry.' The prediction at Bethel and the miracles that confirmed it 'were surely sufficient to have affected and alarmed any heart, not wholly and incorrigibly hardened; and yet they had no effect on Jeroboam.' Worse, he did not even trouble to counterfeit the true worship: 'in his calves... he copied the manner of Egypt; and in the formation of his priesthood, he seems to have gone aside from all models,' taking 'of the lowest of the people' — 'any vagabond that offered was accepted' — men 'too idle to work, and too stupid to learn.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott hears a hint of resistance even so. The emphatic phrase 'whosoever would, he consecrated him' (compare ch. 12:32) 'possibly indicates that, in spite of all that Jeroboam and his prophet could do, there was some difficulty in securing candidates for his unauthorised priesthood.' The witnesses agree on the verse's grim lesson: the fullest evidence — a prophecy fulfilled before his eyes in the rending of the altar, a judgment felt in his own arm, a healing granted to his prayer — could not turn a heart set on its idols. Jeroboam becomes the standing proof that miracles do not of themselves convert, that a will wedded to sin can harden under the very strokes meant to soften it. His obstinacy is not ignorance but choice, and it prepares the reader for the final verdict of v.34, that this persistence was the sin that doomed his house.
34:
'And this thing became sin unto the house of Jeroboam, even to cut it off, and to destroy it from off the face of the earth.' The witnesses read the closing verse as the chapter's theological verdict, and they attend first to the idiom. Gill notes that the idolatries were sins in themselves, but that here 'sin is put for the punishment of sin, as it often is': the king's persistence became 'the causes of punishment... of evil things being inflicted on Jeroboam's family.' Keil and Delitzsch render the sense exactly: 'this obstinate persistence in ungodly conduct was the guilt which had as its natural consequence the destroying of his house from the face of the earth.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads the sentence as the narrator's own summary, 'evidently based on the prophetic denunciation of Ahijah' (ch. 14:9-11) 'and its subsequent fulfilment' in the fall of Jeroboam's line (ch. 15:25-30). The verse thus reaches beyond chapter 13 to knit the whole story of the dynasty together: the calves, the illicit priesthood, the obstinacy under warning — all converge in a single guilt whose end is extinction.

The wesleyan Clarke draws the moral sharply — 'these abominations were too glaring, and too insulting to the Divine Majesty, to be permitted to last; therefore his house was cut off' — before turning aside into his one great digression, a reflection on the civil establishment of religion. A holy priesthood, he argues, 'is a blessing to any state,' inducing 'order, sobriety, and habits of industry,' while 'the profligacy of the clergy, and false principles of religion, are the most likely to unsettle a kingdom'; and he thanks God 'for the religious establishment of his country.' The tangent is Clarke's own, spun from Jeroboam's corruption of worship into a plea for national religion. The witnesses close, however, on the plainer note the verse strikes: that the sin dramatised through the whole chapter — a worship of man's devising, defended against every sign from heaven — was not merely offensive but self-destructive, carrying in itself the seed of a dynasty's ruin. The prophecy that opened at the altar finds here its wider frame; what Josiah would one day do to the stones, God would first do to the house that raised them.
